comment_text.sn11.13:1.3: Mahāli also features in AN 10.47 on the causes of good and bad deeds; in SN 22.60 on Pūraṇa Kassapa’s theory that there is no purification of beings; and in DN 6, where he is called Oṭṭhaddha (“hare-lip”) and shows himself to be familiar with the failed ascetic Sunakkhatta.
comment_text.sn11.13:4.2: In DN 6 he also asks about seeing celestial visions, and the Buddha gives a detailed explanation in terms of meditation attainments.

root_text.sn11.13:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā vesāliyaṁ viharati mahāvane kūṭāgārasālāyaṁ.
root_text.sn11.13:1.3: Atha kho mahāli licchavī yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho mahāli licchavī bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn11.13:5.1: “Sakkañca khvāhaṁ, mahāli, pajānāmi sakkakaraṇe ca dhamme, yesaṁ dhammānaṁ samādinnattā sakko sakkattaṁ ajjhagā, tañca pajānāmi.
root_text.sn11.13:6.1: Sakko, mahāli, devānamindo pubbe manussabhūto samāno magho nāma māṇavo ahosi, tasmā maghavāti vuccati.
root_text.sn11.13:7.1: Sakko, mahāli, devānamindo pubbe manussabhūto samāno sakkaccaṁ dānaṁ adāsi, tasmā sakkoti vuccati.
root_text.sn11.13:8.1: Sakko, mahāli, devānamindo pubbe manussabhūto samāno pure dānaṁ adāsi, tasmā purindadoti vuccati.
root_text.sn11.13:9.1: Sakko, mahāli, devānamindo pubbe manussabhūto samāno āvasathaṁ adāsi, tasmā vāsavoti vuccati.
root_text.sn11.13:10.1: Sakko, mahāli, devānamindo sahassampi atthānaṁ muhuttena cinteti, tasmā sahassakkhoti vuccati.
root_text.sn11.13:11.1: Sakkassa, mahāli, devānamindassa sujā nāma asurakaññā pajāpati, tasmā sujampatīti vuccati.
root_text.sn11.13:12.1: Sakko, mahāli, devānamindo devānaṁ tāvatiṁsānaṁ issariyādhipaccaṁ rajjaṁ kāreti, tasmā devānamindoti vuccati.
root_text.sn11.13:13.1: Sakkassa, mahāli, devānamindassa pubbe manussabhūtassa satta vatapadāni samattāni samādinnāni ahesuṁ, yesaṁ samādinnattā sakko sakkattaṁ ajjhagā.
root_text.sn11.13:13.7: yāvajīvaṁ vigatamalamaccherena cetasā agāraṁ ajjhāvaseyyaṁ muttacāgo payatapāṇi vossaggarato yācayogo dānasaṁvibhāgarato,
root_text.sn11.13:13.9: yāvajīvaṁ akkodhano assaṁ—sacepi me kodho uppajjeyya, khippameva naṁ paṭivineyyanti.
root_text.sn11.13:13.10: Sakkassa, mahāli, devānamindassa pubbe manussabhūtassa imāni satta vatapadāni samattāni samādinnāni ahesuṁ, yesaṁ samādinnattā sakko sakkattaṁ ajjhagāti.

translation_text.sn11.13:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Vesālī, at the Great Wood, in the hall with the peaked roof.
translation_text.sn11.13:1.3: Then Mahāli the Licchavi went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him,
translation_text.sn11.13:2.1: “Sir, have you seen Sakka, lord of gods?”
translation_text.sn11.13:4.1: “But surely, sir, you must have seen someone who looked like Sakka.
translation_text.sn11.13:4.2: For Sakka is hard to see.”
translation_text.sn11.13:5.1: “Mahāli, I understand Sakka. And I understand the things that he undertook and committed to, which enabled him to achieve the status of Sakka.
translation_text.sn11.13:6.1: In a former life, when Sakka was a human being, he was a student named Magha. That’s why he’s called Maghavā, the Bounteous.
translation_text.sn11.13:7.1: In a former life, when Sakka was a human being, he gave gifts ably. That’s why he’s called Sakka, the Able.
translation_text.sn11.13:8.1: In a former life, when Sakka was a human being, he was first to give gifts. That’s why he’s called Purindada, the Firstgiver.
translation_text.sn11.13:9.1: In a former life, when Sakka was a human being, he gave the gift of a guest house. That’s why he’s called Vāsava, the Houser.
translation_text.sn11.13:10.1: Sakka thinks of a thousand things in an hour. That’s why he’s called Sahassakkha, the Thousand-Eyed.
translation_text.sn11.13:11.1: Sakka’s wife is the titan maiden named Sujā. That’s why he’s called Sujampati, Sujā’s Husband.
translation_text.sn11.13:12.1: Sakka rules as sovereign lord over the gods of the thirty-three. That’s why he’s called Devānaminda, the Lord of Gods.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.1: In a former life, when Sakka was a human being, he undertook seven vows. And it was because of undertaking these that he achieved the status of Sakka.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.3: As long as I live, may I support my parents.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.4: As long as I live, may I honor the elders in the family.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.5: As long as I live, may I speak gently.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.6: As long as I live, may I not speak divisively.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.7: As long as I live, may I live at home rid of the stain of stinginess, freely generous, open-handed, loving to let go, committed to charity, loving to give and to share.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.8: As long as I live, may I speak the truth.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.9: As long as I live, may I be free of anger, or should anger arise, may I quickly get rid of it.
translation_text.sn11.13:13.10: In a former life, when Sakka was a human being, he undertook seven vows. And it was because of undertaking these that he achieved the status of Sakka.
translation_text.sn11.13:14.1: A child who respects their parents,
translation_text.sn11.13:14.2: and honors the elders in the family,
translation_text.sn11.13:14.3: whose speech is gentle and courteous,
translation_text.sn11.13:15.1: who’s committed to getting rid of stinginess,
translation_text.sn11.13:15.2: is truthful, and has mastered anger:
translation_text.sn11.13:15.3: the gods of the thirty-three declare
translation_text.sn11.13:15.4: them to be a true person.”

variant_text.sn11.13:0.3: Mahālisutta → mahāli (sattavatapada) suttaṁ (bj); devasuttaṁ (3) (cck); tatiyadevasuttaṁ (sya1ed, sya2ed); devā (3) (pts1ed)
